title: Waste Management Contract and Billing Clarification
resolved_text:
Memorandum
DATE: February 18, 2026
TO: Mayor and City Council
THROUGH: Jackie Bryant, City Manager
FROM: Suzanne Groneman, Environmental Services Manager
Cynthia Esparza, Director
DEPT: Community Engagement & Services
SUBJECT: Waste Management Contract and Billing Clarifications

At the February 11, 2026, meeting of the Reno City Council, the Reno City Council requested clarification of the following matters related to Waste Management:
• Audit Procedures
• Mobile Home Billings
• Periodic Review Status

Franchise Agreement Audit Procedures
Under the Waste Management franchise agreements, the City of Reno can initiate a formal audit through a third party at the City of Reno's discretion and cost. The number of audits cannot exceed two (2) during the base term, and one (1) during any extension term. The base term ends in November 2029. The City of Reno has exercised the City of Reno's review option (explained below) every 5 years, in accordance with the Waste Management franchise agreements, but has not exercised the City of Reno's audit option since prior to 2018. Waste Management representatives are supportive of the audit, which can begin upon direction from staff.

Mobile Home Billings
The Reno City Council presented a concern that mobile home billings were based on city zoning. However, under the Waste Management agreements, mobile home communities have the ability to select from one of three models:

Option 1: Commercial Service (One Container, One Bill)
The mobile home community may opt for a shared dumpster with one consolidated bill for the entire property.

Option 2: Standard Residential Service (Individual Customer Accounts)
Each household may subscribe directly to Waste Management as an individual residential customer. In this model, every resident receives a bill, receives full residential service (trash, recycling, excess-waste stickers, and four annual dumps), and pays the standard residential rate associated with the resident's cart size. Some households may qualify for senior discounts.

Option 3: Master Billed Residential Service (Community Managed Billing)
A property owner or community manager may choose to have all units billed together under a single master account. Waste Management creates sub-accounts for each unit to track service; all units receive the same service level, and the master account is billed at franchise residential rates. Billing is based on 90% occupancy of living spaces. Units may qualify for senior, low-income senior, or side yard service rates with verification. The property owner/manager distributes excess stickers and transfer station passes to residents and verifies tenancy for use.

Periodic Review Status
In June of 2025, staff initiated a comprehensive periodic review of Waste Management's operations and services. The periodic review occurs every five years for two years and is a standard part of the City of Reno's agreement with Waste Management. The periodic review presents an opportunity to evaluate the effectiveness, affordability, and sustainability of the solid waste and recycling services provided to the City of Reno's community. The periodic review period ends in June 2027.

Waste Management Review Process Objective
Ensure the services provided under the Waste Management franchise agreements are cost-effective, reliable, and reflective of community values.

Focus Area Analysis Description Status
Fees, Rates, and Services: Evaluate current fee structure and level of service to ensure the current fee structure and level of service align with community expectations, industry standards, and cost efficiency. Staff reviewed the franchise agreements, ordinances, service levels compared to other jurisdictions, rates, and fees. Initial findings show services are like those in Washoe County and Sparks, rates are set slightly lower than in Washoe County, and ordinance updates are needed. An ordinance to repeal Title 5, Sec. 5.90.070 was referred for a second reading and potential adoption on February 25. The repeal will remove outdated, duplicated, and contradictory language. Franchise fees are set below Sparks and Incline Village, and are the same as Washoe County (prior to Reno City Council action on 2/11/26).

Benchmarking with Other Jurisdictions: Rates and services comparison offered in similar cities to identify potential improvements or innovations in service delivery. In progress.

Reno Direct Calls & Customer Service: Data from Reno Direct will be reviewed to identify trends in customer service issues, such as missed pickups or service disruptions, and evaluate how customer service issues have been resolved. High-level Reno Direct Data is included in the Quarterly Report Memo to the City Council. An in-depth analysis is in progress.

Recycling Performance: Review of recycling rates to help determine whether current recycling programs are effective and what changes might be necessary to increase diversion from landfill. In progress.

Missed Pickups and Resolution Rates: An assessment of service reliability and the responsiveness of Waste Management to customer concerns. Not started.

Composting Survey Results: Analysis of a recent composting survey that closed to better understand community interest and the feasibility of expanding composting services. Survey responses show strong community support for composting services in the City of Reno's community. Over 91% of respondents expressed interest in using compost, and more than 73% of respondents said respondents would consider paying for a pickup service for green and food waste, depending on the cost. The composting survey findings also point to a need for broader education.

Review of Other Jurisdictions' Contracts: Review of partner agencies' contracts to inform any potential changes or improvements to the City of Reno's own agreement with Waste Management. In progress.

ESG (Environmental, Social, and Governance) Reporting: Evaluate Waste Management's ESG data to understand Waste Management's operational sustainability and alignment with Reno's environmental goals. Waste Management ESG goals include reducing absolute Scope 1 and Scope 2 GHG emissions 42% by 2031 (science-based target), target beneficial use of 65% of captured landfill gas by 2026, increase recovery of materials by 60% to 25M tons per year by 2030, including an interim milestone of a 25% increase by 2025, and to produce Renewable Natural Gas from some landfills to fuel a portion of Waste Management's fleet.

Operational Interviews: Conduct interviews with Waste Management's operations personnel to assess field-level challenges and identify opportunities for improvement. Not started.

Transfer Station Utilization: A focused review to understand the current underutilization of the allowance at the transfer station and explore ways to improve efficiency and community access. In progress, but initial findings indicate underutilization of the transfer station allowance, so Staff is working with City of Reno departments to resolve.

Complaint Resolution Performance: Review process and timeliness of complaint resolution to ensure responsiveness, accountability, and complaint resolution practices are meeting community needs. Ongoing.

Local Community Survey: A survey to gauge public satisfaction with existing services and inform recommendations for the future. Similar survey to previous review process. Not started.

fields.authors: Suzanne Groneman, Environmental Services Manager; Cynthia Esparza, Director
fields.tldr: This memo responds to City Council requests from February 11, 2026, clarifying three aspects of the Waste Management franchise agreement: audit procedures, mobile home billing options, and the status of the ongoing periodic review. The City has not exercised its formal audit option since before 2018, though Waste Management is supportive of initiating one. Mobile home communities may choose from commercial, individual residential, or master-billed residential service models. A comprehensive periodic review launched in June 2025 is underway across multiple focus areas, with the review period ending in June 2027.
fields.key_stats: Formal audits limited to 2 during base term (ending November 2029) and 1 during any extension term; last audit exercised prior to 2018. Master-billed residential service billed at 90% occupancy. Composting survey: over 91% of respondents expressed interest in using compost; more than 73% would consider paying for green and food waste pickup. Waste Management ESG targets: 42% reduction in Scope 1 and Scope 2 GHG emissions by 2031; 65% beneficial use of captured landfill gas by 2026; 60% increase in material recovery to 25M tons/year by 2030 (with 25% interim milestone by 2025).
fields.key_decisions: Council action on 2/11/26 affected franchise fee levels. An ordinance to repeal Title 5, Sec. 5.90.070 was referred for second reading and potential adoption on February 25, 2026, to remove outdated, duplicated, and contradictory language. Staff awaits direction to initiate a formal third-party audit of Waste Management.
fields.action_items: Staff awaiting Council direction to initiate formal Waste Management audit. Ordinance repealing Title 5, Sec. 5.90.070 scheduled for second reading and potential adoption on February 25, 2026. Staff working with City departments to address underutilization of transfer station allowance. In-depth Reno Direct customer service data analysis in progress. Multiple periodic review focus areas not yet started: Missed Pickups and Resolution Rates, Operational Interviews, and Local Community Survey.

projects: Waste Management Franchise Agreement Periodic Review

2018 (last audit exercised prior to this year)
2026 (Waste Management landfill gas beneficial use target)
